title: Chapter 5: r/NoSleep
content: **I think my employers are gaslighting me or I'm losing my mind.**
📌 **r/NoSleep** · Posted by **u/Maeve_Bly** · 🕐 3:17 AM GMT · ⬆️ 47.2k
Okay. Deep breath. I've never posted here before but I've been lurking for months and honestly I just need someone to tell me if I'm crazy because I can't tell anymore.

I took a job as a live-in governess. Beautiful old estate, two kids (twins, eight years old, absolutely angelic on paper), insane salary — like, "is this a typo" salary. I should've asked more questions. I have a psychology degree from Edinburgh and I fancy myself fairly sharp, but I saw the number and the manor house and the grounds and I thought: fairy tale.

First week was perfect. The kids were lovely. The housekeeper was warm in that British "I'll be rude to you but I'll also make you tea" way. The house itself is — I don't know how to describe it. It *breathes*. High ceilings, long hallways, rooms that seem to change temperature when you're not looking. I chalked that up to old construction.
Then things started moving.

Not dramatic Poltergeist stuff. Subtle. My book would be on the nightstand when I'd left it on the bed. My rain boots by the back door when I'd put them in the closet. A glass of water on my bedside table that I didn't pour. Little things. The kind of things that make you think *did I do that and forget?* Which — I have a psych degree. I KNOW that's exactly how gaslighting works. You make someone doubt their own perception until they can't trust anything.
Then the kids started with the comments.

We were walking by the lake last week and the girl — Flora — she stops dead and points at the water and says, "She's watching you again." I asked who. She smiled and said, "The lady by the water. She likes you." I looked. Nobody there. The boy — Miles — just laughed and said Flora was being dramatic.
But here's the thing. I've seen her.

Not clearly. Not enough to be sure. But twice now, out by that lake, there's been a figure. Standing at the tree line across the water. Woman, dark hair, wearing something old-fashioned. Both times I looked away for a second and she was gone. Both times it was dusk.

The housekeeper — Mrs. G — she won't talk about it. I asked about the history of the house and she changed the subject so fast I got whiplash. I asked if anyone had died here and she said "not to concern myself with that" which is NOT a no.

I took this photo yesterday. It's blurry because my hands were shaking and I'll be honest I wasn't trying to take a picture of the lake I was trying to take a picture of the figure I saw and by the time I got my phone out she was gone but I snapped it anyway.
[📷 IMG_4683.jpg — Lake at dusk, tree line reflected in dark water.Blurry, phone camera grain. Fig tree and willow on far shore, grey English sky.]

Something is wrong here. Either my employers are running some kind of elaborate psychological experiment on me, or the housekeeper knows more than she's saying, or I am genuinely losing my grip on reality.

I have a psychology degree. I know what gaslighting looks like. I know how to test for it — document everything, look for the pattern, find the lever. But what if I'm wrong? What if I'm not being gaslit? What if the thing I'm experiencing is something my degree didn't prepare me for?
I don't know what I'm asking for here. Validation? A reality check? Someone to tell me I should pack my bags and leave?
I can't leave. The kids need me. And there's something else — something I haven't figured out how to articulate yet. Like the house doesn't want me to go.
That sounds insane. I know.
I'm posting this at 3 AM because I can't sleep. The hallway light keeps turning itself on.
### Comments
**u/TrueCrimeJunkie_88** · 🕐 3:24 AM GMT · ⬆️ 12.4k

OP, I'm not trying to alarm you but I went down a rabbit hole. You said "Bly" — you're at Bly Manor, yeah? Essex countryside, estate, lake? If that's the one then you need to look up what happened to Peter Quint. He was a valet at that estate, died on the grounds — found by the lake, head injury, officially ruled accidental but the locals had a LOT to say about it. This was maybe a decade ago? There was an inquest. I'll try to find the article. Something about the owner at the time too, but the records are weirdly sparse. DM me if you want what I've got. And be careful.
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/SkepticalSherlock** · 🕐 3:31 AM GMT · ⬆️ 9.8k

I'm going to go against the grain here and say: look for cameras. Seriously. Gaslighting requires access and control. If someone is moving your stuff, they need to get into your room. If they're making you think you're seeing things, they need a way to stage it. Check the light fixtures, the smoke detectors, any small holes in walls or furniture. Check if the hallway light is on a smart switch. Don't assume supernatural when "creepy employer with a nanny cam" is statistically far more likely. Post an update after you sweep.
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/CottagecoreQueen** · 🕐 3:38 AM GMT · ⬆️ 6.1k

Okay I know this is NoSleep and everyone's being serious but can we talk about how GORGEOUS this setup is?? Old manor, lake, twins, fog, mysterious housekeeper — this is literally my dream job. I would die to live in a house like that. The woman by the lake is probably just a local taking a walk. English countryside people are built different, they wander around in Victorian-looking rain gear all the time. Honestly OP you might just be experiencing Old House Vibes™. Embrace the aesthetic! 🔥🍂

**u/TherapistThrowaway** · 🕐 3:44 AM GMT · ⬆️ 8.7k

Clinical psychologist here (lurker, this isn't medical advice). OP, a few things stand out: you moved to a new country (assuming you're not from Essex based on the Edinburgh degree), started a high-stress live-in role, are socially isolated, and are experiencing onset of perceptual disturbances within the first month. This is a textbook stress response presentation. The sleep disruption, the hypervigilance, the ambiguous stimuli being interpreted as threatening — it all tracks. The mind fills in gaps when it's exhausted. I'm NOT dismissing your experience. I'm saying: before you conclude it's external, please rule out the physiological. When did you last sleep a full 8 hours? When did you last eat a proper meal away from the house? When did you last talk to someone who isn't part of this environment? The hallway light turning on at 3 AM is your brain being awake during a hypnagogic transition, not evidence of a haunting. Take care of your baseline first.
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/Detective_Z** · 🕐 3:51 AM GMT · ⬆️ 7.3k

You said "I can't leave. The kids need me." But you also said you took the job for the salary. So which is it? Because those are two very different reasons to stay in a situation that's making you question your sanity. I'm not trying to be a dick — genuinely asking — what's keeping you there? Is it the money, the kids, or something you're not saying? Because "the house doesn't want me to go" isn't a reason. That's a feeling. And feelings like that, in a situation like this, are usually installed. Someone or something made you feel that. Ask yourself when that feeling started and what was happening right before it.
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/deep_water_fred** · 🕐 4:02 AM GMT · ⬆️ 5.4k
OP zoom in on the lake reflection — is that you standing behind yourself?

I'm not joking. I downloaded the image and zoomed on the water reflection under the far tree line. There's a silhouette. It looks like it's facing you. Same height, same posture. But you said you were alone. I don't know, maybe it's a trick of the light and the branches, but I've been staring at it for five minutes and it's creeping me out. Can anyone else see it or am I losing it too?

**u/BlyWatcher** · 🕐 4:09 AM GMT · ⬆️ 4.1k

I've never commented on this sub before. I made this account ten minutes ago because I saw this post and my blood went cold. OP, listen to me carefully. The locals don't go near Bly. I grew up in the next village over. We were told as children not to walk the estate paths, not to fish in that lake, not to look at the house from the road. I thought it was just small-town superstition until I was fifteen and a girl from our school went to work there as a mother's helper. She wrote letters home for three weeks. Then the letters stopped. Her parents went to the house and the housekeeper told them she'd left — packed her bags in the night and caught a train. No forwarding address. No goodbyes. This was a girl who called her mum every Sunday without fail. She didn't leave. She *disappeared*. That was twelve years ago. No body. No trail. The police didn't investigate because there was no evidence of foul play. Just a girl who walked into Bly Manor and ceased to exist. OP — what was the name of the woman who worked there before you? The one whose job you took?
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/ParanormalDebunker** · 🕐 4:14 AM GMT · ⬆️ 3.9k

Classic NoSleep. Every detail calibrated for maximum gothic atmosphere, conveniently blurry photo, "I have a degree so I'm credible" framing, kids who speak in cryptic pronouncements. Not buying it. Good writing though — the "house doesn't want me to leave" line is a nice touch. 7/10, would read the novelization.
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/MidnightLibrarian** · 🕐 4:18 AM GMT · ⬆️ 3.2k

Hi OP. Former archivist for the Essex county records (retired 2022). I can't confirm or deny anything about Bly specifically because a lot of estate records from that area are sealed or privately held. But I will say this — I once pulled a file that was marked "inquest" and found that the pages had been removed. All of them. Just an empty folder with a reference number and a date range that matched the period you're all discussing. That kind of thing doesn't happen by accident. Someone wanted that record clean. Whether it was the estate, the family, or someone else — I can't say. But records don't empty themselves. Be careful what you dig for. The people who emptied that file are probably still alive.
📎 *Reply* · *Award* · *Share*
**u/LakeWalker_Essex** · 🕐 4:26 AM GMT · ⬆️ 2.8k

I walk the public footpath that runs along the eastern edge of the Bly estate. I've done it for twenty years. I stopped walking the section that borders the lake two years ago. I won't tell you why. I'm not a superstitious person. But I stopped, and I haven't gone back, and I don't intend to. That's all I'll say.

### Author's Response
**u/Maeve_Bly** (OP) · 🕐 4:33 AM GMT · ⬆️ 11.6k
Okay. I did not expect this many responses. I'm reading all of them.
@SkepticalSherlock — I'll check for cameras tomorrow. That's actually a really good idea and I feel stupid for not thinking of it.
@TherapistThrowaway — I hear you. You're probably right. But "probably" isn't the same as "definitely," and I'd rather be embarrassed than dead.
@deep_water_fred — What the hell are you talking about? I'm going to look at the photo again. I was alone. I know I was alone.
@BlyWatcher — I don't know her name. They never told me her name. Which, now that you say it like that—
@TrueCrimeJunkie_88 — DM me everything.
📌 **This post has been archived. Last updated: 4:33 AM GMT.**
⬆️ **47,243 upvotes** · 💬 **1,847 comments** · 🏆 **6 awards**
🏷️ *Locked · Front Page · r/All Rising*

She had not typed the name Peter Quint. She had not typed the name Miss Jessel. She had described a woman by the lake and a dead man's shadow and the house that breathed, and she had posted a photo taken with shaking hands at dusk, and forty-seven thousand people had clicked the arrow to say *I see you.*

But deep_water_fred had seen something else. In the reflection — the dark water mirroring the far tree line, the grey English sky, the willow's weeping silhouette — there was a shape. Standing where no one should have been standing. Facing the camera. Same height. Same posture. As if the lake had copied her and the copy had stayed.

She examined the photo again, zoomed in until the pixels fragmented into color and noise. Her hands were steady now. The hallway light was off. Somewhere in the manor, a door closed — softly, deliberately, the way a person closes a door when they don't want to wake the children.

She had not mentioned Peter's name. She had not mentioned Jessel's name. She had left out the suitcase under the bed and the frantic annotations in red ink and Flora standing in the dark asking *do you know where Miss J is?* She had left out so much.
The question the internet should have asked — the one that mattered — was not *what is in the reflection?*
It was: *who took the photo?*
Maeve had said she raised her phone when she saw the woman by the lake. But her two hands were visible in the photo's shadow at the bottom of the frame. Both of them. Empty. At her sides.

She posted the photo at 3:17 AM. The hallway light turned itself on seconds later. And in the intervals between those two facts, in the silence of an old house that breathes and keeps its records empty and its doors soft, someone or something had held her phone, framed the shot, and pressed the shutter.
She hadn't taken the picture.
She hadn't been alone.
And she still didn't know who — or what — had been standing close enough to feel her breathing, close enough to press a thumb to glass, close enough to *choose* what the internet would see.
The post was on the front page now. Forty-seven thousand witnesses, and none of them had asked the right question.
Not yet.
*End of Chapter 5*
